summary: Stellar meat sequence with Truckin' → The Other One → El Paso
blurb: The second‑set meat – Truckin' into Drums, The Other One, El Paso and back into The Other One ending on Wharf Rat – is a highlight. First set bursts with energy via Greatest Story Ever Told, China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider and Sugaree. Performance is praised despite the recordings being poor.
review: April 7, 1972 at Wembley Empire Pool captures one of the high points of Europe ’72. While both audience tapes are described as “horrendous” – muffled, distorted and plagued by drop‑outs – the band’s playing shines through. Jerry Garcia delivers searing solos on Truckin' and The Other One, his tone “on fire,” while Phil Lesh lays down a driving bass line with a memorable “caution tease” in the same suite. Bob Weir’s vocals crackle on Loser and Big Boss Man, adding to the set’s urgency. The meat sequence – Truckin' → Drums → The Other One → El Paso → The Other One → Wharf Rat – is repeatedly called one of the best of the tour, with Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming described as “other‑worldly.” Keith’s keyboards carve out melodic space during the jam, and Pigpen’s organ adds texture on China Cat Sunflower. Even though you’ll have to endure sub‑par audio, this show is a must‑listen for anyone chasing that classic Europe ’72 intensity.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Both recordings, despite poor audio, consistently praise the performance – especially the Truckin' → The Other One → El Paso sequence and Garcia’s solos. Positive language such as “stellar,” “very nice and powerful,” and “other‑worldly” appears across analyses, outweighing negative comments that focus solely on tape quality.

best_recording.reason: Most complete audience capture of the full show despite poor fidelity; reviewers note it as the reference tape for this performance.

key_highlights: Epic meat sequence: Truckin' → Drums → The Other One → El Paso → The Other One → Wharf Rat.
First‑set energy with Greatest Story Ever Told, China Cat Sunflower > I Know You Rider and Sugaree.
Garcia’s fiery solos and Phil’s driving bass make the second set a tour‑high point.
Recording quality is terrible (muffled, drop‑outs), but the performance still shines.

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia’s tone and solos are repeatedly praised, especially on Truckin' and The Other One, delivering fire‑brand improvisation.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh provides a solid, driving groove with a notable “caution tease” during The Other One, anchoring the jam.
band_performance.Bob: Weir’s vocals are described as “on fire,” adding excitement on Loser and Big Boss Man.
band_performance.Brent: Keith’s keyboards carve melodic space in The Other One and support the jam; Pigpen’s organ texture is heard on China Cat Sunflower.
band_performance.Billy: Bill Kreutzmann’s drumming is highlighted as “other‑worldly,” driving the intense meat sequence and delivering a standout drum break into The Other One.
